question_text:
(a) Consider the following function at point $a=(1,2,3)$, [cite: 26]
$$f(x,y,z)=x^{3}y^{2}z.$$ [cite: 28]
(i) Determine a formula for the directional derivative for f at a along the direction of a unit vector u. [cite: 29]
(ii) Calculate the direction and rate of steepest ascent for f at a. [cite: 30]
(iii) Calculate the direction and rate of steepest descent for f at a. [8] [cite: 31, 42]
(b) Find and classify all the critical points of the following function: [cite: 32]
$$f(x,y)=(y-2)x^{2}-y^{2}.$$ [8] [cite: 33, 43]
(c) In the partial differential equation, [cite: 34]
$$x\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}+y\frac{\partial f}{\partial y}=0,$$ [cite: 35]
change the variables from (x, y) to $(u,v)$ where [cite: 36]
$$u=tan^{-1}(y/x)$$ and $$v=ln\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}},$$ [cite: 38]
and $(x,y)\ne(0,0)$ [9] [cite: 37, 44]

(a) Find the equation of the plane which passes through the point (-2, 1,7) and is perpendicular to vector $\vec{a}=(1,-8,5)$. [4] [cite: 47, 48]
(b) Consider a circle of radius $R=1$ centered at the origin in the (x,y)-plane. [cite: 49]
A particle moves along the circle at a constant speed. [cite: 50]
Prove, showing all steps, that the particle's velocity is always orthogonal to its acceleration. [7] [cite: 51, 58]
(c) Consider $\vec{r}=(x,y,z)$ where $(x,y,z)\ne(0,0,0)$. [cite: 52]
Calculate, showing all steps, $$div(\frac{\vec{r}}{r})$$ where $r=|\vec{r}|$ [8] [cite: 52, 53, 59]
(d) Consider the vector field $\vec{F}(x,y,z)=(x(z-y),y(x-z),z(y-x))$. [cite: 54]
Find the outward flux of $\vec{F}$ through a unit sphere centered at the point $P=(1,-1,2)$. [6] [cite: 54, 60]

(a) Consider the double integral [cite: 63]
$$I=\iint_{D}\frac{1}{(x^{2}+y^{2})^{3/2}}dxdy,$$ [cite: 65]
where the domain D is bounded by $x = 1$, $x = 2$, $y = 0$ and $y=\sqrt{3}x$. [cite: 66]
(i) Express the integral as repeated integrals using Cartesian coordinates. [cite: 67]
(ii) Express the integral as repeated integrals using polar coordinates. [cite: 68]
(iii) Calculate the double integral you have written using one of the two coordinate systems. [10] [cite: 69, 84]
(b) Consider the following triple integral [cite: 70]
$$J=\iint_{S}e^{(x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2})^{3/2}}dxdydz,$$ [cite: 71]
where the domain S is bounded by the following two surfaces: $$z=\sqrt{3(x^{2}+y^{2})},$$ $$z=\sqrt{4-(x^{2}+y^{2})}.$$ [cite: 71, 72]
(i) Express the integral as repeated integrals using cylindrical coordinates. [cite: 73]
(ii) Express the integral as repeated integrals using spherical coordinates. [cite: 74]
(iii) Calculate the triple integral you have written using one of the two coordinate systems. [cite: 75]
(iv) Using the result of (iii), or otherwise, calculate the following integral: $$K=\iint_{S}(e^{(x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2})^{3/2}}+sin(x)cos(y))dxdydz.$$ [cite: 77, 78]
(v) Using the result of (iii), or otherwise, calculate the following integral: $$L=\iint_{T}e^{(x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2})^{3/2}}dxdydz,$$ [cite: 78, 79]
where the domain T is defined as $$T=\{(x,y,z):(x,y,z)\in S \text{ and } y\ge0\}.$$ [15] [cite: 80, 81, 85]

(a) Consider points $P_{1}=(1,1)$, and $P_{2}=(2,6)$ in the (x, y)-plane. [cite: 89]
Let $C_{1}$ be a straight line between the points $P_{1}$ and $P_{2}$ and $C_{2}$ be an arc of the parabola $y=2x^{2}-x$ between the points $P_{1}$ and $P_{2}$. [cite: 90]
Find the difference $I_{2}-I_{1}$ between the following integrals: [cite: 91]
$$I_{1}=\int_{C_{1}}(x+y)^{2}dx-(x-y)^{2}dy,$$ and $$I_{2}=\int_{C_{2}}(x+y)^{2}dx-(x-y)^{2}dy.$$ [12] [cite: 92, 93]
(b) Demonstrate, showing all steps, that the vector field $\overline{F}(x,y,z)=(2x+yz,xz,xy+2z)$ is a gradient field. [cite: 94]
Hence, find a potential function $f(x,y,z)$ for $\vec{F}$ by evaluating line integrals. [13] [cite: 95]

(a) Suppose that the positive real-valued function $z=z(x,y)$ is defined implicitly by the following equation: [cite: 138, 139]
$$cos(xy)+ln(z)=yz-1/2.$$ [cite: 140]
Find the partial derivatives $z_{x}$ and $z_{y}$ in terms of x, y and z. [cite: 141]
Further, calculate their values at the point $(x,y,z)=(\pi,1/2,1).$ [7] [cite: 142, 153]
(b) Write the equation for the tangent line to the intersection of the following two surfaces: [cite: 143]
$$xy+yz+zx=11$$ and $$xyz=x+y+z$$ [cite: 144]
at the point $a=(1,2,3)$. [9] [cite: 145, 154]
(c) Using the method of Lagrange multipliers, find the maximum and minimum values of the function: [cite: 146]
$$f(x,y,z)=x+z,$$ [cite: 147]
where $(x,y,z)$ lies on the surface determined by the equation [cite: 148]
$$x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}=1$$. [9] [cite: 149, 155]

(a) (i) Give the definition of the cross product of two vectors, $\vec{a}=(a_{1},a_{2},a_{3})$ and $\vec{b}=(b_{1},b_{2},b_{3})$. [cite: 157, 158]
(ii) Prove that $\vec{a}\times\vec{b}=-\vec{b}\times\vec{a}.$ [5] [cite: 159, 161]
(b) Prove that the integral $I=\int_{C}y^{3}cos~3x~dx+y^{2}sin~3xdy$ is path-independent. [6] [cite: 160, 162]
(c) Find the work of the vector field $\vec{F}:\mathbb{R}^{2}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{2}$, where $\vec{F}(x,y)=(-\frac{\pi}{y},\frac{2\pi}{x})$ along the arc, given by the section of the unit circle that starts at the point (0, 1) and ends at the point $(-1,0)$. [8] [cite: 164, 165]
(d) (i) Calculate the divergence of the following vector field: [cite: 166]
$$\vec{F}=(4xz,-y^{2},yz)$$. [cite: 167]
(ii) Find the curl of the following vector field: [cite: 168]
$$\vec{F}=(x^{2}+yz,y^{2}+zx,z^{2}+xy)$$ at the point (1,2,3). [6] [cite: 169, 170, 174]

Consider the triple integral [cite: 175]
$$I=\iint_{S}zdxdydz,$$ [cite: 177]
where the domain S lies in the first octant of the real space, i.e. where $x,y$ and z are non-negative, and is bounded below by the surface $z=(x^{2}+y^{2})^{2}$ and bounded above by the surface $x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}=2$. [cite: 178, 179, 180]
(a) Calculate the integral $I$ using cylindrical coordinates. [10] [cite: 181, 183]
(b) Express the integral $I$ as repeated integrals using Cartesian coordinates. [3] [cite: 182, 184]
(c) Express the integral $I$ as repeated integrals using spherical coordinates. [6] [cite: 185, 186]
(d) Using the result of (3a), or otherwise, calculate the following integral: [cite: 188]
$$J=\iint_{T}(z+x~sin(y^{2}))dxdydz,$$ [cite: 189]
where the domain T is enclosed by the two surfaces $z=(x^{2}+y^{2})^{2}$ and $x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}=2$. [6] [cite: 190, 191, 192]

(a) Find the value of the integral $I=\int_{C}(2x+\frac{1}{y})dx+(2y-\frac{x}{y^{2}})dy$ along an arbitrary smooth curve C, which starts at the point $A(1,1)$ and ends at the point $B(2,2)$ without crossing the x-axis. [15] [cite: 193, 198]
(b) Compute the value of the line integral [cite: 194]
$$\oint_{C}(x^{2}+y)dx+2xdy$$ [cite: 195]
using Green's theorem, where C is the counterclockwise-oriented triangle with vertices (0,0), (1,0) and (0, 1). [cite: 196]
In your solution please include an explanation as to why Green's theorem can be applied here. [10] [cite: 197, 199]

(a) Consider the function [cite: 228]
$$f(x,y,z)=e^{xy^{2}z^{3}}$$. [cite: 229]
Calculate: [cite: 230]
(i) The direction and rate of steepest ascent for the function at the point (1, 1, 1). [cite: 232]
(ii) The direction and rate of steepest descent for the function at the point (1, 1, 1). [cite: 233]
(iii) The directional derivative of the function at the point (1, 1, 1) in the direction from the point (1, 1, 1) to the point (2, -1, -1). [7] [cite: 234, 244]
(b) Find the tangent plane to the surface, described by the following equation: [cite: 235]
$$x^{2}+y^{3}+z^{4}=3$$ at the point (1, 1, 1). [5] [cite: 236, 237, 245]
(c) Find and classify all the critical points of the following function: [cite: 238]
$$f(x,y)=xy^{2}-3x^{2}-y^{2}+2x+2$$. [7] [cite: 239, 246]
(d) Find the volume of the solid that is enclosed by the surfaces: $z=0$, $z=y^{2},$ $y=x^{2}$ and $x=y^{2}$ [6] [cite: 240, 247]

(a) Give the definition of the triple scalar product (also known as the mixed product). [cite: 250]
Prove that for any $\vec{a}$ $\vec{b}$ and $\vec{c},$ [cite: 251]
$$\vec{a}\cdot(\vec{b}\times\vec{c})=\vec{c}\cdot(\vec{a}\times\vec{b})=\vec{b}\cdot(\vec{c}\times\vec{a})$$. [6] [cite: 252, 253]
(b) Consider the curve C given by the following parametrization: [cite: 254]
$$x=sin^{2}(t),\quad y=1-cos(t),\quad z=2t$$. [cite: 255]
Find the unit tangent vector $\vec{T}$ to the curve C at point $M_{0}$ corresponding to $t=t_{0}=\frac{\pi}{2}$ [6] [cite: 256, 262]
(c) Consider a vector field $\vec{F}$ defined on the whole 3-dimensional space and let $\vec{G}=c\vec{F},$ where $c\in\mathbb{R}$ is a constant. [cite: 257]
Prove, showing all steps, the following statement: if $\vec{F}$ is a conservative vector field, then the field $\vec{G}$ is also conservative. [5] [cite: 258, 263]
(d) Consider a counterclockwise-oriented closed contour C consisting of two parts $C_{1}$ and $C_{2}$ [cite: 259]
The curve $C_{1}$ is defined by its parametrization as $C_{1}:x(t)=R~cos(\pi t),y(t)=R~sin(\pi t)$ for $0\le t\le1$ and the curve $C_{2}$ is defined as $C_{2}:x(t)=Rt,y(t)=0$ for $-1\le t\le1,$ where $R\in\mathbb{R}$ and $R>0.$ [cite: 259]
Compute the line integral $\oint_{C}\vec{F}\cdot d\vec{r}$ of the vector field $\vec{F}=(cos~x-\frac{y^{3}}{3},\frac{x^{3}}{3}+sin~y)$ along the contour C. [8] [cite: 259, 260, 261, 264]

Consider the triple integral as follows: [cite: 269]
$$J=\iint_{T}xydxdydz,$$ [cite: 271]
where the domain T is the tetrahedron with its vertices at (0, 0, 0), (2, 0, 0), (2, 2, 0) and (2, 0, 2). [cite: 272]
(a) Express the integral as repeated integrals using: [cite: 273]
(i) Cartesian coordinates, [cite: 274]
(ii) Cylindrical coordinates, and [cite: 275]
(iii) Spherical coordinates. [18] [cite: 276, 284]
(b) Calculate the integral using any one of the three coordinates, using the result obtained in (a), or otherwise. [3] [cite: 277, 285]
(c) Using the result of (b), or otherwise, calculate the following integral: [cite: 278]
$$K=\iint_{S}(xy+xy^{2}sinh(z))dxdydz,$$ [cite: 279]
where the domain S is the union of the domain T and its image in the Oxy plane. [4] [cite: 280, 286]

(a) Consider a function $f(x,y,z):\mathbb{R}^{3}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}$ where $f(x,y,z)$ is twice differentiable in a spatial domain $D\subset\mathbb{R}^{3}$ [cite: 289]
The Laplace equation written for the function $f(x,y,z)$ in the domain D is: [cite: 289]
$$\Delta f\equiv\frac{\partial^{2}f}{\partial x^{2}}+\frac{\partial^{2}f}{\partial y^{2}}+\frac{\partial^{2}f}{\partial z^{2}}=0.$$ [cite: 290]
(i) Demonstrate, showing all steps, that $\Delta f=div(grad(f))$ [cite: 291]
(ii) Let $f(x,y,z)\equiv f(r)=\frac{1}{r},$ where $r=\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}}$, and let the domain D be defined as $D:x>0,y>0,z>0$ [cite: 292, 293, 294]
Is the function $f(r)$ a solution to the Laplace equation in the domain D? [cite: 294]
Explain your answer, showing all steps. [12] [cite: 295, 303]
(b) Consider the vector field [cite: 296]
$$\vec{F}=((\frac{x^{3}}{5}+\frac{z^{2}y^{4}}{10}+\frac{z^{5}}{15}),(\frac{x^{5}z}{25}+\frac{y^{3}}{5}+\frac{x^{3}z^{2}}{10}),(\frac{yx^{5}}{10}+\frac{x^{5}y^{3}}{15}+\frac{z^{3}}{5}))$$ [cite: 297]
and the spherical shell $\Omega$ defined as : $a^{2}\le x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}\le b^{2},$ where $a,b\in\mathbb{R}$ and $0<a<b$. [cite: 298]
Find the surface integral $\int\vec{F}\cdot d\vec{S}$ over the surface of the spherical shell $\Omega$. [13] [cite: 299, 304]

(a) Find the direction of the tangent to the intersection of the two surfaces: [cite: 342]
$$xy+yz+zx=5$$ and $$xyz=2$$ [cite: 343]
at the point (1, 1, 2). [6] [cite: 344, 359]
(b) Consider the function: [cite: 345]
$$f(x,y,z)=x+y+z+e^{xyz}.$$ [cite: 346]
Calculate: [cite: 348]
(i) The gradient of the function at the point $(0,1,1)$. [cite: 349]
(ii) The direction and rate of steepest descent for the function at the point (0, 1, 1). [cite: 350]
(iii) The directional derivative of the function at the point (0, 1, 1) in the direction from the point (0, 1, 1) to the point (-2, -1, 2). [7] [cite: 351, 360]
(c) Using Lagrange multipliers, find the maximum and minimum values of the function: [cite: 352]
$$f(x,y,z)=x+2y+3z$$ [cite: 353]
subject to the constraint: [cite: 354]
$$x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}=14$$ [12] [cite: 355, 361]

(a) Consider the following double integral [cite: 363]
$$I=\iint_{D}\frac{x}{x^{2}+y^{2}}dxdy,$$ [cite: 365]
where D is the triangular region with vertices at (0, 0), (1, 0) and (1, 1) in Cartesian coordinates. [cite: 366]
(i) Express the double integral as repeated integrals in polar coordinates, integrating with respect to $r$ first. [cite: 367]
There is no need to evaluate the integral in this step. [cite: 368]
(ii) Express the double integral as repeated integrals in polar coordinates, integrating with respect to $\theta$ first. [cite: 369]
Again, there is no need to evaluate the integral in this step. [cite: 370]
(iii) Calculate the integral using the results obtained in (i) or (ii) or otherwise. [10] [cite: 371, 376]
(b) Consider the region S, which is inside the spherical surface $x^{2}+y^{2}+z^{2}=4$ but outside the cylindrical surface $x^{2}+y^{2}=1$. [cite: 372]
(i) Express the volume V of the region as repeated integrals using cylindrical coordinates. [cite: 373]
(ii) Express the volume V of the region as repeated integrals using spherical coordinates. [cite: 374]
(iii) Calculate the volume V using either of the two coordinate systems, using the results obtained in (i) or (ii) or otherwise. [15] [cite: 375, 380]

(a) Consider the force $\vec{F}=(sin(y)e^{x}-py)\hat{i}+(cos(y)e^{x}-p)\hat{j},$ $p\in\mathbb{R}$ and find the work W when a particle is moved by the force $\vec{F}$ once around a closed contour C formed by the x-axis and the upper semi-circle $(x-a)^{2}+y^{2}=a^{2},$ $y\ge0$, $a>0,$ where C is oriented counterclockwise. [8] [cite: 383, 389]
(b) Consider $\vec{r}=(x,y,z)$ and let $\vec{r}_{0}$ be the position vector of a point $P_{0}=(x_{0},y_{0},z_{0})$ [cite: 384]
Calculate, showing all steps in your solution, in the domain D, where $\vec{q}=\vec{r}-\vec{r}_{0},$ $q=|\vec{q}|$, and $\mathbb{D}=\mathbb{R}^{3}\backslash\{P_{0}\}$: [cite: 384, 385, 387]
$$\nabla\cdot(\frac{\vec{q}}{q})$$ [9] [cite: 386, 390]
(c) Consider the vector field $\vec{F}=2~sin(x)y\hat{i}+x^{3}e^{z}\hat{j}-cos(x)yz^{2}\hat{k}$ and find, showing all steps, the flux of $\vec{F}$ through the surface S of the cube $0\le x\le1$, $0\le y\le1$, $0\le z\le1$, where a normal vector to the surface S is pointing outwards. [8] [cite: 388, 391]

(a) Consider a circle of radius $R=1$ centred at the origin in the (x, y)-plane. [cite: 397]
A particle moves along the circle at a constant speed. [cite: 398]
Prove that the acceleration vector and the position vector of the particle are collinear at any point of the circle. [5] [cite: 399, 401]
(b) Evaluate, showing all steps in your solution, the integral: [cite: 400]
$$\int_{C}yds,$$ [cite: 402]
where C is an arc of the curve $y=\sqrt{2x}$ between the points (0,0) and $(1,\sqrt{2})$ [7] [cite: 400, 405]
(c) Demonstrate, showing all steps, that the vector field $\vec{F}=(2x+y^{2}z)\hat{i}+2xy\hat{zj}+(xy^{2}-1)\hat{k}$ is a gradient field. [cite: 403]
Hence, find the potential function f by computing line integrals. [13] [cite: 404, 406]
